name: Gravel route through the upper Ebro
name_original: Ruta gravel por el alto Ebro
region: National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón
description:
Spain's great river gushes between the mountains of Cantabria and makes its way through the limestone, carving gorges and sinuous gorges. The landscape of the upper Ebro is lush and spectacular and that is why we want to propose this gravel bike route along the tracks of the natural park of the sickles of the upper Ebro and the Rudrón.

The route not only immerses you in beautiful beech and oak forests, but also constantly overlooks the riverbed with unforgettable views. In addition, you pass through towns that seem straight out of a story and are home to ancient bridges or a spring of water with a waterfall in the center, such as Orbaneja del Castillo.

The Cantabrian mountains can be explored from mid-spring to mid-autumn, so you can enjoy the best of all three seasons. The route is suitable for gravel bikes, apart from the desire to pedal and space on your device for photos, remember to bring supplies and some spare parts.
region_intro: Gravel biking around National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón offers diverse terrain for cyclists. The park is characterized by deep limestone canyons carved by the Ebro and Rudrón rivers, contrasting with expansive high-altitude moorlands. This landscape provides a mix of gravel roads and trails that wind through varied ecosystems, from riparian forests to open heathlands. The region's geological formations and river valleys create a dynamic environment for no traffic gravel bike trails. Best no traffic gravel bike trails around National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón The most popular no traffic gravel bike trail is Gravel route through the upper Ebro, a 30.8 miles (49.6 km) trail that takes 4 hours 1 minute to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through the Ebro river valley. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Hoz de Orbaneja del Castillo – El Tobazo Waterfall loop from Parque Natural Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón, a difficult 40.8 miles (65.7 km) path. This route explores the dramatic canyon landscapes and passes through the village of Orbaneja del Castillo. Local gravel bikers also love the Trespaderne – Oña Historic Town Center loop from Villarcayo de Merindad de Castilla la Vieja, a 43.9 miles (70.6 km) trail leading through varied terrain and historic towns, often completed in about 5 hours 40 minutes. Gravel biking around National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón is defined by deep river gorges, high moorlands, and diverse forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, with all listed routes being challenging. The routes in National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.0 stars from more than 4 reviews. More than 60 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no traffic gravel bike trails are available in National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón?
A: There are 5 dedicated no-traffic gravel bike trails in the National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón featured in this guide. These routes offer a challenging yet rewarding experience through the park's stunning landscapes.

Q: What makes gravel biking in National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón unique?
A: The park offers a unique blend of dramatic limestone canyons carved by the Ebro and Rudrón rivers, expansive moorlands, and lush riparian forests. You'll ride through diverse ecosystems, often with the Ebro River as a constant companion, providing a truly immersive and scenic gravel biking experience.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of gravel biking in this park?
A: The gravel bike trails in this guide are generally considered difficult. They often feature significant elevation changes and varied terrain, making them best suited for experienced gravel bikers looking for a challenging adventure. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1502699953/gravel-route-through-the-upper-ebro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel route through the upper Ebro&lt;/a&gt; covers nearly 50 km with over 800 meters of elevation gain.

Q: What do other gravel bikers enjoy most about the trails here?
A: The komoot community rates the gravel biking experience in National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón highly, with an average score of 3.0 stars. Riders frequently praise the breathtaking canyon views, the tranquility of the no-traffic routes, and the opportunity to explore the region's unique geological formations and rich biodiversity.

Q: Are there any circular gravel routes in the National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón?
A: Yes, several routes in this guide are circular. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23740653" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hoz de Orbaneja del Castillo – El Tobazo Waterfall loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38152647" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oña Historic Center – Oña Historic Town Center loop&lt;/a&gt; are excellent options for riders who prefer to start and end their journey in the same location.

Q: Are there routes that pass by waterfalls or other natural attractions?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23740653" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hoz de Orbaneja del Castillo – El Tobazo Waterfall loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you near the famous urban waterfall of Orbaneja del Castillo. You can also explore highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2581741" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Turquoise Pools and Waterfalls of Orbaneja del Castillo&lt;/a&gt; and the enigmatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2577310" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo Azul de Covanera&lt;/a&gt;, known for its turquoise waters.

Q: What kind of landscapes will I encounter on these gravel trails?
A: Expect a diverse range of landscapes. You'll ride through deep, winding limestone gorges with vertical cliffs, expansive high-altitude moorlands, and lush riparian forests featuring holm oak, gall oak, juniper, beech, poplars, willows, and alders. The contrast between the dramatic canyons and the serene riverbanks is a defining feature.

Q: Are there opportunities to see wildlife while gravel biking?
A: Yes, the park is a haven for wildlife. Keep an eye out for griffon vultures soaring above the canyons – it's home to one of the largest colonies on the Iberian Peninsula. You might also spot Egyptian vultures, peregrine falcons, and eagle owls. Along the rivers, otters and brown trout are common, while roe deer and badgers inhabit the forested areas.

Q: What is the best season for gravel biking in National Park Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón?
A: Spring and autumn are generally the best seasons for gravel biking in the park. The temperatures are milder, and the natural scenery is at its most vibrant, with blooming wildflowers in spring and colorful foliage in autumn. Summer can be hot, especially in the canyons, while winter may bring colder temperatures and potentially challenging conditions.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites accessible from the gravel routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38274293" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trespaderne – Oña Historic Town Center loop&lt;/a&gt;, can lead you near charming villages and historical sites. The park also features Romanesque architecture, such as 12th-century churches at San Pedro de Tejada and Escalada, offering cultural stops along your ride.

Q: Where can I find parking for these gravel bike trails?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. Many of the routes start near villages or designated parking areas within the park. It's advisable to check the specific route details on komoot for recommended starting points and parking information before your trip. For example, routes often begin near towns like Villarcayo de Merindad de Castilla la Vieja or Hozabejas.
tips: The Orbaneja del Castillo gorge is one of the most impressive landscapes of the Ebro. The water has carved and dug the limestone, forming a spectacular karstic landscape. On the crests of the ravine the stone formations look like battlements. Here is the Cueva del Agua, a cavity where an aquifer emerges whose stream crosses the town and cascades down to join the current of the Ebro.

Spectacular bridge of medieval origin that crosses the Ebro River as it passes through Pesquera de Ebro, a charming town that captivates with its beauty and tranquility. Built in stone, its elegant arches and robust structure bear witness to the passing of time. Crossing it is delving into history and enjoying impressive views of the river.
If you ride near the Ebro you cannot miss stopping at one of the viewpoints to observe its meanders. The views are spectacular! Totally recommended.

waypoints.name: Medieval Bridge of Pesquera de Ebro
